Tafseer:
1. Analysis of words and clarification of verses: {Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise} (1)
(The importance and wisdom of this verse): This verse has already appeared at the beginning of Surah Al-Hadid, where its implications were explained. The point of special attention here is that both the beginning and the end of this Surah conclude, with a very minor difference, with this same verse. This indicates that the realities described in this Surah support and confirm the implications of this verse. It is as if this Surah proves its original claim, which was described in the previous Surah, and also provides evidence of those attributes of Allah that are described in this introductory verse. The reality that this verse reminds us of is that this entire universe, through its glorification, sanctification, servitude, and submission, bears witness that its Creator is completely pure and free from any defect, weakness, or taint of association (shirk). He holds power and authority over everything. No one can obstruct His will, and there is wisdom in His every action. For this reason, the correct attitude for His servants is to entrust themselves to Him, rely upon Him, obey His commands, fear Him, and place their hopes in Him. He is the true disposer of affairs; nothing can move from its place without His permission. The deep-rooted establishment of this reality in the mind gives rise to true faith, which is the source of all resolve and strength. Conversely, any rupture in this belief allows hypocrisy, disbelief, and polytheism to creep into the heart, spreading corruption in every sphere of knowledge and action.

{He is the One Who expelled those who disbelieved among the People of the Book from their homes at the first gathering. You did not think they would leave, and they thought their fortresses would defend them against Allah. But Allah came upon them from where they did not expect, and cast terror into their hearts so they destroyed their houses with their own hands and the hands of the believers. So take warning, O people of vision!} (2).
(The treachery of Banu Nadir and their fate): According to commentators, {those who disbelieved among the People of the Book} refers to the Jews of Banu Nadir, who lived near Medina. According to the account in Bukhari, their story in brief is that although they had entered into a peace treaty with the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), six months after the Battle of Badr, in violation of the treaty, they colluded with the enemies of Islam and were guilty of a failed conspiracy to assassinate the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). In punishment for this crime, he ordered them to leave Medina. At first, they were prepared to comply with this order, but later the famous hypocrite, Abdullah ibn Ubayy, instigated them, saying, "Two thousand of my men are with you, and Quraysh and Ghatafan will also support you; refuse to leave." Fooled by Abdullah ibn Ubayy's words, they refused to leave. Eventually, the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) led an army against them. At that time, neither Banu Qurayzah, nor Quraysh, nor Ghatafan supported them. Out of necessity, they had to comply with the Holy Prophet's order. Out of grace, he allowed them to take whatever belongings they could carry on their camels. Consequently, they took as much of their belongings as they could carry and went to Khaybar and Adhri'at. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) took possession of their remaining properties and estates. {At the first gathering (Hashr)}: This means their expulsion was the first gathering of its kind. Within this lies a warning that they would face a similar gathering later on; indeed, during the caliphate of Hazrat Umar (may Allah be pleased with him), they were expelled from there as well, and the greatest gathering—the gathering of the Day of Judgment—is yet to come.
(Address to the hypocrites): {You did not think they would leave, and they thought their fortresses would defend them against Allah.} This address is directed at those hypocrites mentioned in the previous Surah. It is said: "You had great reliance on their strength, population, and the support of their allies, and you assumed they could never be displaced from here. They themselves were also very proud of their fortresses and strongholds, thinking no one would dare to confront them face-to-face. But look how their pride was humbled."
(The mentality of the arrogant): In the phrase {against Allah}, according to Arabic grammar rules, a word is omitted in the genitive construction, i.e., "against the punishment of Allah" or "against the torment of Allah" or "against the grasp of Allah." The meaning is that when they built fortresses and strongholds, they fell into the delusion that they were safe not only from humans but also from the grasp of God. This is an accurate picture of the mentality of the arrogant. Those who gain power and glory in this world think that no breach can ever occur in their fortress. Even if a servant of God warns them of God's grasp, they cannot comprehend how God could enter their secure fortress!
(The attack): {But Allah came upon them from where they did not expect.} That is, they had left no place in their estimation for God to enter their fortresses, but God fell upon them from where they had no inkling. {And He cast terror into their hearts.} This points to where God launched His attack against them. He said that they built walls of brick and stone around themselves and thought they were beyond God's reach, but Allah did not even find it necessary to remove their walls; instead, He directly cast terror into their hearts. The effect of this was that, despite having fortresses and strongholds, they were so terrified that they demolished their own homes with their own hands. It should be kept in mind here that real strength does not lie within weapons and the walls of fortresses, but in the hearts, which is born of faith in Allah. If this strength is present, a soldier fights even without a sword, and if it is absent, even the largest quantity of atomic weapons is useless; rather, there is a danger that these things might become the source of one's own destruction instead of the enemy's. {They destroyed their houses with their own hands and the hands of the believers.} This is a picture of their state of terror, showing that the houses they had built with so many desires and aspirations were being demolished by their own hands. {Yukhribuna} (they destroy) is used here to paint a vivid picture. We have pointed out above that they were permitted to take as much of their belongings as their camels could carry. Benefiting from this permission, they attempted to dismantle the beams, rafters, doors, and windows of their houses to load them onto their camels. In addition to greed for wealth, their animosity toward Muslims was also at play; therefore, they must have tried to render whatever was left behind useless so that the Muslims could not benefit from it. {And the hands of the believers}: The Muslims must have assisted in this dismantling so that these mischief-makers would leave their vicinity as quickly as possible. Furthermore, subsequent verses show that the Muslims also cut down some of their orchards for military necessity. {So take warning, O people of vision!}: This is an invitation to take warning from this event, addressed to all those who possess the capacity to reflect, so that they may see how every word of the Quran proved true. See how those who opposed Allah and His Messenger were humiliated, and how the party of Satan was defeated by the party of Allah. If, even after this, some people wish to maintain friendship with the enemies of Allah and His Messenger, let them try and see what the end result will be.

{And if Allah had not decreed exile for them, He would have punished them in this world, and in the Hereafter they will have the punishment of the Fire} (3). That is, Allah showed them leniency by sentencing them only to exile. The requirement of His wisdom was to suffice with this warning, so that if they had any capacity to take warning, they might reform their behavior. If this were not the case, Allah would have sent down a decisive torment upon them in this world, similar to the torments that befell Ad, Thamud, and Pharaoh, which wiped out their stories completely.
{And in the Hereafter they will have the punishment of the Fire}: That is, if they do not benefit from this leniency, the matter will not end with this exile alone; rather, the punishment of Hell awaits them in the Hereafter, which will make up for any remaining account.

{That is because they opposed Allah and His Messenger. And whoever opposes Allah—then indeed, Allah is severe in penalty} (4). That is, this wrath befell them because they opposed Allah and His Messenger, and the divine law is that those who oppose Allah face His severe retribution. A noteworthy point in this verse is that in the phrase {And whoever opposes Allah}, the words "and His Messenger" have been omitted. This indicates that enmity with the Messenger is, in reality, enmity with Allah, and whoever stands up to oppose Allah should think carefully about what his end might be!

2. Subject matter of the subsequent verses 5 to 10:
Moving slightly away from the main thread of the discourse, the upcoming verses serve as a parenthetical statement to answer some objections and doubts raised by the Jews and hypocrites regarding the event of Banu Nadir. One objection was raised by the Jews, claiming that the Muslims had cut down many of their fruitful palm trees. Although these trees were cut down due to military necessity, the Jews and their sympathizers tried to portray this as spreading corruption in the land (ifsad fil-ard), whereas the Muslims claim to be reformers. The purpose of this objection was to make the call of Islam doubtful in the eyes of the people, presenting it not as a call to faith, but as a struggle for power in which the name of religion is used merely to deceive people (we seek refuge in Allah).
An answer to an objection of this nature has already passed in Surah Al-Hadid, which was also raised by the People of the Book.
The second question was raised by the hypocrites regarding the properties and estates left behind by Banu Nadir. They argued that, like the spoils of war (ghanimah), one-fifth should be set aside and the rest distributed among the soldiers. However, the Quran classified this not as ghanimah, but as Fay (assets acquired without fighting), and decreed that all of it would go to the public treasury. It was to be used to help the poor, the needy, and especially those early Emigrants (Muhajirun) who were driven from their homes and deprived of their properties solely for the sake of religion. In this context, the selflessness and generosity of the Ansar and the early Muhajirun are praised, highlighting how they are open-hearted toward their emigrant brothers and prefer the needs of others over their own. This is the behavior that befits the people of faith. Read the verses in this light.
3. Analysis of words and clarification of verses:
(A timely answer to an objection): {Linah} refers to a fruitful date palm tree. This is a fitting reply to an objection raised by the Jews and their allies. When the Muslims marched against Banu Nadir, they had to cut down some trees in their orchards due to military necessity. The Jews made this a tool for propaganda against Islam, saying: "The Muslims claim they have stood up to reform the land, but they have cut down our excellent, fruit-bearing trees! Is cutting fruit-bearing trees a work of reform? This is clear corruption in the land!"
The mention of {or left them standing} apparently seems unnecessary, since their objection was to the cutting of the trees and not to leaving them. However, its mention sheds light on a specific aspect of their objection that could not have been illuminated otherwise. This is that when they raised this objection, they must have said, to make it effective: "If the Muslims only needed wood for immediate necessity, they could have cut down such-and-such trees whose removal would not have caused much loss. But they left those standing and cut down these fine, fruitful trees, which shows that this action was not taken out of an unavoidable necessity, but out of a spirit of revenge to cause economic ruin, which is pure corruption, not reform."
{It was by permission of Allah and so He can disgrace the defiantly disobedient (Fasiqin)}: This answers the objection, but instead of addressing the objectors directly, it addresses only the Muslims: "Do not pay any attention to these self-proclaimed advisors regarding which trees you should have cut and which you should have left. Whatever you did was in the presence of the Messenger and under his guidance, which means it was done by the permission of Allah. And when it is done by Allah's permission, no one can be more knowing of wisdom and utility, nor better at distinguishing between reform and corruption than Allah."
{So He can disgrace the Fasiqin}: That is, Allah gave you this permission to disgrace these treaty-breakers and traitors, so they would see with their own eyes that the trees they had planted with great hopes were being cut down in front of them, yet they were so helpless they could not utter a word. The word 'Fasiq' is used here in the sense of a traitor and treaty-breaker, a meaning in which this word is used in various places in the Quran. The reason for not addressing the objectors and critics here is that this was an absurd objection raised merely to vent their frustration. Therefore, they did not deserve to be addressed. However, by addressing the Muslims and validating their action, Allah caused grief to the traitors and warned the hypocrites not to use it as a source of whispering doubts. As for the question of whether Muslims can destroy the orchards and fields of enemies during war, it is not a major issue. If war necessity demands it, they can even demolish their houses, let alone their orchards and fields. However, if there is no war necessity, it is not permissible for Muslims to damage even the smallest thing belonging to the enemy.

{And what Allah restored to His Messenger from them—you did not spur for it any horses or camels, but Allah gives His messengers power over whom He wills, and Allah is over all things competent} (6).
(Answer to a second question): This is the answer to the second question that arose regarding the properties left behind by Banu Nadir, which the hypocrites intensified due to their greed for wealth. Their demand was that since this was wealth obtained from the enemy, it should be distributed among the soldiers after setting aside one-fifth, just like the spoils of war. A similar distribution had been carried out shortly before at the Battle of Badr. Keeping that in mind, they made this demand. However, the Quran did not accept this demand because of the distinct difference between the two situations. At Badr, the warriors had to fight a regular battle in which they used their own weapons, camels, and horses, whereas in this instance, no such thing occurred; rather, intimidated by the awe of Allah's Messenger, the enemy vacated their territory on their own. Due to this difference, it was decreed that this wealth has the status of Fay, which Allah Himself delivered to His Messenger from His enemies. Therefore, it belongs entirely to Allah and His Messenger—or in other words, to the Islamic state—and will be spent on the collective welfare of Islam and Muslims.
(Fay wealth and its ruling): From this phrase {what Allah restored} (ma afaa Allah), a permanent term in Islamic public finance was born, namely "Fay," which refers to wealth obtained from the enemy without fighting. 'Fay' literally means 'to return,' meaning that Allah takes this wealth from the usurpers and returns it to its rightful owners. The phrase {you did not spur for it any horses or camels} indicates that the reason soldiers were given a share of the spoils of war was because they had to use their personal weapons, horses, and camels in battle, and even had to carry their own provisions. Since the situation has now completely changed, whatever is obtained from the enemy in this era will have the status of Fay, whether obtained through war or peace.
{But Allah gives His messengers power over whom He wills, and Allah is over all things competent}: It has already passed in Surah Al-Mujadilah, verse 21, that {Allah has written: 'I will surely overcome, I and My messengers.'} Under this divine sunnah, victory is decreed for the Messenger. For this victory, the Messenger of Allah is not dependent on anyone's help except his Lord, and his Lord is competent over all things. If He wills, He can grant him victory over the largest country of the enemies without any army. If He seeks help from the believers, it is not because He is dependent on their help, but to open the path of spiritual success for them and to distinguish the sincere from the hypocrites.

("What Allah has bestowed on His Messenger from the people of the cities...") (7).

After clarifying the distinction between Ghanimah (spoils of war) and Fay' (properties acquired without active combat), the use of Fay' is explained here. It belongs entirely to Allah, the Messenger, the close relatives of the Messenger, orphans, the needy, and the wayfarer. This means those who fought do not have a share in it. The avenues of expenditure mentioned here have already been discussed in Surah Al-Anfal regarding Ghanimah. Therefore, we will only highlight specific points here. For details, please refer to the tafseer of Surah Al-Anfal.

In "For Allah and the Messenger," as far as Allah is concerned, He is not in need of any wealth or possessions. His share, in reality, returns to His servants, and the Islamic government, acting as a trustee, spends it on the needy and the collective welfare of the Muslims. Similarly, the share of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is not in his capacity as a messenger, but rather as the head of the Islamic state. His sustenance as a Messenger was guaranteed by Allah Himself, as explicitly stated in the Quran. However, when the Islamic state came into being and the burden of its duties fell upon his shoulders, it became necessary that he should have the right to take from the public treasury according to his needs. Since this right was by virtue of his position as the head of state, it returned to his successor after his passing. It was not of the nature of personal property, which would raise the question of inheritance after him. The Prophets (peace be upon them) leave behind the inheritance of knowledge and religion, not wealth and property.

"And for the close relatives" refers to those relatives of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) whose maintenance was his responsibility. This was, in fact, part of his personal needs. Its nature was also not that of personal property to be inherited by his family after him. Just as no specific family has a monopoly over Islam, no family has ever had, nor can have, a monopoly over the Islamic government or any part of its treasury (Bayt al-Mal). Such ideas were spread among Muslims by mischief-makers under Jewish influence, but they have no foundation. Mentioning the rights of orphans, the needy, and wayfarers immediately after the relatives of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) clarifies their high status in the Islamic system. Allah mentioned their rights alongside those of the Prophet's relatives. The foremost responsibility of an Islamic government is the care and patronage of those who are resource-less in society. Its other duties come after this. If a government ignores this primary need and spends the revenues of the treasury on other requirements—no matter how important those requirements might be from a social or cultural standpoint—it is guilty of breach of trust regarding the rights of the actual beneficiaries. There is absolutely no justification for an Islamic government to spend even a single penny of the public treasury on useless displays and luxury. Any government that does so is not an Islamic government but a satanic one. It is due to such governments that the calamity of communism descended upon this world, which deemed the very concept of private property a crime—indeed, the root of all crimes—and shed rivers of blood to eradicate this "crime."

"So that it may not circulate only among the rich among you." The word daulah means circulation. "Daalat al-ayyam" means "time rotated." From this comes mudaawalah, which is also used in the Quran: "And these days [of varying fortunes] We alternate among the people" (Al-Imran: 140). The meaning is that the rights of the poor, orphans, and resource-less have been secured in the public treasury so that wealth does not merely circulate among the rich, but also finds a path to reach the poor.

From this, a fundamental principle of Islamic economics is clarified: Islam does not favor wealth being concentrated within a specific class; rather, it intends for wealth to flow toward those classes that, due to natural weaknesses or lack of resources, cannot fully participate in the struggle to acquire it. For this purpose, it has urged individuals to spend as much as possible (infaq), making this voluntary spending the greatest means of their spiritual development. It has also legally separated a portion of every wealthy person's property as the right of the poor, placing it under the custody of the government.

(Warning to the Objectors) -- "And whatever the Messenger has given you - take; and what he has forbidden you - refrain from. And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is severe in penalty." At the end is a warning and threat: whatever the Messenger gives you, take it, and whatever he forbids you from, refrain from it. Do not use anything as an excuse for objection, criticism, or secret conspiratorial talks. Fear Allah constantly, and remember that Allah is severe in punishment. This threat and warning indicate that the questions answered here were raised by the hypocrites, and their purpose was not genuine inquiry but their habitual objection and criticism. If the question had been from sincere Muslims seeking to find the truth, there would have been no occasion for such a warning.

(Deducing a General Rule from a Specific Command): Although the status of the Messenger clarified here—that whatever he gives you, take it, and whatever he forbids, refrain from—has a specific context, the rule deduced from it is entirely general. That is, in every matter of life, the commands and prohibitions of the Messenger must be obeyed without question, because the status of a Messenger, as clarified in the Quran, is that of an obligatory-to-obey guide appointed by Allah. He said: "And We did not send any messenger except to be obeyed by permission of Allah" (An-Nisa: 64). Thus, this segment has two meanings: one specific and the other general. In its specific meaning, it is connected to the preceding context, while in its general meaning, it serves as a comprehensive principle of Islamic Shariah.

("For the poor emigrants who were expelled from their homes and their properties...") (8).

(A Temporary Avenue for Fay' Wealth): After stating the general avenues for the distribution of Fay', this verse points to a specific, urgent need of the time that deserved the equal attention of all Muslims: aiding the emigrants (Muhajirun) who were then arriving in Madinah from all directions, having been uprooted from their homes. Naturally, the responsibility of resettling them and revitalizing their economic lives fell upon this small Islamic society that was just beginning to emerge in Madinah. This situation required that Fay' wealth be placed under government control so that resources would be available to solve such national issues.

(Definition of the Muhajirun): In describing these emigrants, Allah says that they were forced out of their homes and properties, and they accepted this deprivation of property and expulsion from their homelands solely to seek the bounty and pleasure of Allah and to aid Allah and His Messenger. Therefore, they deserve that their religious brothers help them with complete generosity and open-heartedness. The phrase "seeking bounty from Allah and [His] pleasure" points to the spiritual provisions of faith, relying on which these emigrants set out from their homes and properties without worrying about what they would eat or where they would shelter. And "aiding Allah and His Messenger" points to the beloved objective for which they took this great risk—namely, they desired to stand shoulder-to-shoulder with the Messenger of Allah so they could be ready to aid Allah and His Messenger at every step.

"Those are the truthful." It is stated that these people are indeed truthful in their claim of faith, as they sacrificed everything for the pleasure of Allah and the support of the Messenger, unlike the hypocrites who claim to believe but are unwilling to bear even a minor hardship in the way of Allah. They simply want to receive benefits without any effort. Regarding these hypocrites, it is said later in Verse 11 of this Surah: "And Allah testifies that they are liars."

("And [also for] those who were settled in the homeland and [adopted] the faith before them...") (9).

(Commendation of the Generosity of the Ansar and the Early Emigrants): This is a praise of the generosity and open-heartedness of the Ansar and the early emigrants, showing that their hearts are not constrained by the waves of refugee caravans arriving to share their spoils and Fay'. Instead, they welcome them with open arms, and rather than feeling any envy or jealousy over the assistance given to them, they prefer them over themselves, even if they themselves are in need.

(A Rule of Arabic Grammar): "Who settled in the homeland and [adopted] the faith." This structure is similar to classical Arabic phrases where a verb appropriate for the second object is omitted but understood from the context (such as "I fed him straw and [gave him] water"). If we assume an omitted verb appropriate for "faith" (such as ahkamu - made firm), the full meaning would be: "those who established their homes and made firm their faith before them."

Its primary referents are obviously the Ansar, because they already possessed homes and enjoyed the blessing of faith. However, in my view, it also includes the early emigrants who had already migrated to Madinah and for whom Allah had provided a satisfactory arrangement for living and livelihood. The words "before them" point toward this. It is the status of the early emigrants that they preceded the later emigrants in both faith and migration. As for the Ansar, they certainly had precedence in terms of possessing homes, but regarding faith, it cannot be said that they preceded all the emigrants. At most, we can say that they had collective precedence over the later emigrants. If we include the early emigrants alongside the Ansar, the words "those who settled in the homeland and faith" apply perfectly. Together, they are prior to the newcomers both in terms of residence in Madinah and acceptance of Islam. It is stated that they love those who migrate to them and welcome them with open hearts. Their hearts are not straitened by the continuous arrival of emigrant caravans or by the fact that the wealth they could have received is being spent on them. Instead, they generously prioritize the needs of others over their own.

The purpose of this praise is to show that believers should be mutually sympathetic, generous, and self-sacrificing. This is, in a sense, a mirror held up to the hypocrites who demanded that the wealth left by Banu Nadir be distributed among the people like spoils of war. In this mirror, they are shown how a Muslim behaves generously toward his brother, so that they might feel some shame for their selfishness.

"And whoever is saved from the covetousness of his soul - those are the successful." This is both a glad tiding of success for these Ansar and early Muhajirun, and a warning against a very dangerous disease of the human soul. Shuhh means greed, avarice, and selfishness. Attributing it to the soul indicates that it is an inherent inclination of the human self, but the warning to protect oneself from it shows that it is a dangerous urge. If a person cannot control it, it ruins their Hereafter. I have translated it as "selfishness" to highlight this dangerous aspect. A Hadith clarifies this: "Beware of covetousness (selfishness), for indeed, it destroyed those before you. It commanded them to commit injustice, so they did; it commanded them to transgress, so they did; and it commanded them to sever family ties, so they did."

(A Question and its Answer): An important question arises regarding the context of this verse: what is its purpose here? Our commentators generally believe that just as the previous verse mentions the Muhajirun's share in the Fay', this verse indicates that the Ansar also have a share in it. However, this interpretation is difficult to accept. The mention of the Muhajirun was necessary because the government was facing a critical situation regarding them, which required having resources at its disposal to manage the crisis. Thus, their mention served as a justification for placing the Fay' under state control. Why then would the Ansar need to be mentioned in this context? No one had any doubt that the Ansar were not excluded from this wealth. When all the orphans, poor, and needy were declared eligible, it was obvious that the poor and needy among the Ansar were also included. Why then was there a need to mention the Ansar specifically, and that too with the description "those who settled in the homeland and faith," which highlights their self-sufficiency rather than their need?

In our view, the commentators have not fully grasped the context of this verse. This verse is not concerned with whether the Ansar have a share in the Fay' wealth. No such question existed, there was no reason for it to arise, and answering it would serve no purpose. What is being expressed here is that the Ansar and the early Muhajirun (who were already settled and established in faith) do not view their migrating brothers as an economic threat—unlike the hypocrites—but welcome them with love and exhibit self-sacrifice. This attitude is the true requirement of Islamic brotherhood, which every sincere Muslim must adopt.

("And [also for] those who came after them, saying, 'Our Lord, forgive us and our brothers...' ") (10).

(Commendation of the Later Emigrants): After describing the attitude of the Ansar and the early emigrants, the attitude of the later emigrants is described here. Their hearts are filled with sincerity and love for their brothers who preceded them in faith and migration. They do not feel envy that those who arrived earlier secured the available resources and became established, while they themselves are currently deprived. Instead, they sincerely pray for themselves and their brothers: "Our Lord, forgive us and our brothers who preceded us in faith, and put not in our hearts any resentment toward those who have believed. Our Lord, indeed You are Kind and Merciful."

In "and put not in our hearts any resentment toward those who have believed," there is a subtle indication: "Let not Satan create any malice or envy in our hearts when we see their better circumstances compared to ours. Rather, through Your kindness and mercy, keep our hearts filled with love and affection for them." Here, one should keep in mind the disease of the hypocrites' hearts, which was described earlier by the word shuhh (covetousness). The meaning is that the hearts of these sincere and pure companions are entirely free from such diseases.

4. Content of Verses 11 to 17:

It appears that even after witnessing the fate of Banu Nadir with their own eyes, the eyes of these Jewish-allied hypocrites were not opened. Following that event, they began backing Banu Qurayzah, the only remaining Jewish tribe in the vicinity of Madinah. Naturally, after the incident of Banu Nadir, Banu Qurayzah feared that their turn might be next. It is possible they began conspiring with their sympathizers. The hypocrites reassured them, telling them not to fear: "If you are expelled, just like Banu Nadir, we will leave with you. We will never obey anyone regarding you, and if you are fought, we will fight alongside you." The Quran reassures the Muslims that these hypocrites are cowards and liars. They will neither accompany them in exile nor help them in war. And if they do support them, they will be defeated and will never be able to face anyone again. Along with this, Banu Qurayzah is warned that if they fall for their deception, their fate will be the same as that of Banu Nadir, and the hypocrites will abandon them like Satan, who says: "Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of the worlds" (Al-Hashr: 16). Recite the verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses:

"Have you not seen those who were hypocrites saying to their brothers who disbelieved among the People of the Scripture, 'If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will not obey, concerning you, anyone ever; and if you are fought, we will surely aid you.' But Allah testifies that they are liars." (11)

(The Hypocrites' Conspiracy with Banu Qurayzah): The address "Have you not seen" (Al-am tara) is used here to express wonder. The meaning is: look at these claimants of faith; on one hand, they claim to believe, and on the other, they are cultivating close ties of love with those of the People of the Book who rejected the call and messenger-ship of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The phrase "to their brothers" makes it clear that the hypocrites mentioned here were originally from among the Jews. For the sake of their personal interests, they had outwardly left the Jews, but Jewish sentiments had not left their hearts. For their political and economic benefits, they stood with the Muslims, but their real sympathy lay with their brothers. It must be remembered that the majority of the hypocrites discussed in these chapters were originally Jewish, and their behavior is under discussion. Very few of the Arabs who accepted Islam became hypocrites, and the nature of their hypocrisy was different. The attitude of that group will be discussed later in Surah Al-Mumtahanah.

"Those who disbelieved among the People of the Scripture.": Commentators have faced confusion in identifying the specific group of Jews referred to here. However, I am satisfied that this refers to the Jewish tribe of Banu Qurayzah. The reason is that three major Jewish tribes lived in the vicinity of Madinah: Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qurayzah. Banu Qaynuqa's matter had already been resolved. The fate of Banu Nadir was described earlier in this Surah. After that, only Banu Qurayzah remained, living some distance from Madinah. Therefore, the conspiracy of the hypocrites can only be with them. Their end came immediately after the Battle of the Trench, the details of which have been covered in Surah Al-Ahzab.

"If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will not obey, concerning you, anyone ever; and if you are fought, we will surely aid you.": These hypocrites began reassuring them that if an attempt was made to expel them as was done with Banu Nadir, they would leave with them, and they would never heed anyone's command or advice regarding them. And if war occurred, they would aid them. This shows that Banu Qurayzah feared their turn was near, and it also suggests they did not fully trust the promises of the hypocrites. They had already observed in the case of Banu Nadir that these hypocrites were only verbal champions. Thus, the hypocrites had to emphasize and solemnize their oath to reassure them, even assuring them that they would not care about anyone else. This is clearly an allusion to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims. The meaning is: even if we have to cut off relations with these people for the sake of our covenant with you, we will do so.

"But Allah testifies that they are liars." With the same emphasis with which the hypocrites assured Banu Qurayzah of their support, Allah declared them to be liars. We have already explained that "Allah testifies" carries the meaning of an oath.

"If they are expelled, they will not leave with them, and if they are fought, they will not aid them. And [even] if they should aid them, they will surely turn their backs; then they will not be aided." (12)

(Refutation of the Hypocrites): The Quran refuted every single one of their promises, stating that when the time comes, they will not prove true in any of their words. If they are expelled, they will not go with them; if they are attacked, they will not support them; and even if they do stand up to help, they will be defeated and flee. This is exactly what happened: when the time came, no one was of any help to them. The details of their exemplary end have already been described in the commentary of Surah Al-Ahzab.

(No One Supported Banu Qurayzah at the Critical Moment): Briefly remember here that, like Banu Nadir, they had also entered into a treaty of peace and security with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). During the Battle of the Trench, the infamous Jewish leader Huyayy ibn Akhtab incited them to break the treaty. He convinced them that the Quraysh and the Ghatafan were attacking the Muslims with their full forces, and if they also joined, the Muslims would be finished. Deceived by him, Banu Qurayzah broke the treaty and joined the invaders. The outcome of the Battle of the Trench is well known. As soon as the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was free from it, he turned toward Banu Qurayzah and besieged them. At that moment, no one supported them. Ultimately, they were forced to leave their fate in the hands of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (may Allah be pleased with him). The details of this decision have passed in Surah Al-Ahzab. Through Sa'd, Allah uprooted them. "Then they will not be aided," meaning all doors of hope would be closed to them. This would be Allah's decisive grasp, and those caught in Allah's grasp can find no helper.

("You [believers] are more intense in hesitancy [i.e., fear] in their breasts than Allah. That is because they are a people who do not understand.") (13).

(Those Who Do Not Fear Allah Fear Human Beings): That is, they will not stand up to support their brothers against you because the fear of you in their hearts is greater than the fear of Allah. They commit opposition and disobedience to Allah both secretly and openly, yet they will never have the courage to stand up against you. "That is because they are a people who do not understand." It is indeed strange that they are daring toward God yet terrified of you, but those whose intellect is compromised behave this way—fearing the whip rather than the master of the whip. If they had even a spark of intellect and faith, they would realize that since they have angered their real Master, how long can they protect themselves by hiding from His servants? It should be noted here that a heart is deprived of understanding clear realities of intellect and nature when Allah seals it as a punishment for violating obvious truths. Regarding the hypocrites, it is said: "That is because they believed and then disbelieved; so their hearts were sealed, and they do not understand" (Al-Munafiqun: 3).

("They will not fight you all except within fortified cities or from behind walls. Their violence [i.e., enmity] among themselves is severe. You think they are together, but their hearts are diverse. That is because they are a people who do not reason.") (14)

That is, do not give any weight to the empty threats of these cowards. They do not possess the courage to face you in an open, organized battle. Forget about taking the field; even if they are attacked, they will not have the courage to come out and defend themselves, but will try to protect themselves by barricading themselves in fortified towns or behind the walls of their houses. It is clear that fortified or besieged fighting is primarily defensive; offensive warfare is out of the question in such a state, and even defensively, it is the weakest form of warfare, chosen only out of sheer helplessness. Regarding the Jews, remember that while they were very cunning in inciting the Quraysh and their allies against the Muslims, they never came out openly into the field. Whenever the Muslims attacked them, they always attempted to defend themselves from behind fortifications and walls, resulting in failure and humiliation.

(The Real Weakness of Cowards): "Their enmity among themselves is severe. You think they are together, but their hearts are diverse." The meaning is that they could only have the courage to face you in an open field if their hearts were united. As far as animosity toward Islam is concerned, they are indeed united, which might deceive an observer into thinking they possess unity. But the reality is quite the opposite. Except for the negative goal of opposing Islam, their hearts are filled with severe hatred and animosity toward one another. Deep enmity exists among the various Jewish clans, and their friendship with the Quraysh and pagan tribes is purely superficial, limited only to their opposition to Islam. How can such an unstable and superficial alliance have the strength to stand against those whose hearts have been joined together by faith in Allah? "That is because they are a people who do not reason." This points to the root cause of their disease: they do not use their intellect. Instead of reflecting on realities, weighing them seriously, and facing them with resolve, they have handed over their reins to their desires. When a nation replaces intellect with its desires as its guide, it falls into such confusion of thought and is destroyed.

("[They are] like the example of those shortly before them: they tasted the bad consequence of their affair, and they will have a painful punishment.") (15).

(The Example of Banu Nadir's Fate): This example is given to those whom the hypocrites were inciting by promising: "If you are expelled, we will leave with you, and if you are attacked, we will fight alongside you." It is said that if they make a wrong move under their deception, they should remember that their end will be the same as those who recently tasted the consequence of their mischief. "Those shortly before them: they tasted the bad consequence of their affair." Generally, commentators have taken this to refer to the Quraysh, seeing it as an allusion to the Battle of Badr, where the Quraysh were defeated.

Although this could refer to the Quraysh, or even to Banu Qaynuqa as Ibn Kathir understood, I have already submitted that these verses detail the conspiracy that the hypocrites began with Banu Qurayzah after the exile of Banu Nadir. Therefore, in my view, this refers to the fate of Banu Nadir. The first reason is that the example of Banu Nadir was very fresh, as indicated by the word "shortly" (qariba). The second is that the most effective example for a Jewish group would be another Jewish group. "And they will have a painful punishment," meaning they will face such a fate in this world, and a painful punishment awaits them in the Hereafter. It is clear here that in this world, Banu Qurayzah faced a far more severe end than Banu Nadir, and what awaits them in the Hereafter is known to Allah alone.

("[The hypocrites are] like the example of Satan when he says to man, 'Disbelieve.' But when he has disbelieved, he says, 'Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of the worlds.'") (16).

(The Example of the Hypocrites with Satan): This is the example of the hypocrites who were inciting Banu Qurayzah, saying: "If you are expelled, we will leave with you; if you are fought, we will help you; and we will accept no pressure from anyone in this matter." But they are the brothers of Satan and will adopt his behavior. Just as Satan incites man to disobey God, and when the person commits the crime under his deception, Satan turns around as a moralist, blames him, and abandons him. Similarly, these hypocrites are encouraging them today, but when they take action and the consequences unfold, these inciting devils will run away with their tails between their legs, completely unwilling to accept responsibility for the outcome.

(Satanic Mischief in the Battle of Badr): In Surah Al-Anfal, Verse 48, in connection with the events of the Battle of Badr, it is described that when the forces of the disbelievers and the Muslims faced each other, Satan first encouraged the disbelievers, saying: "No one can overcome you today from among the people, and indeed, I am your protector." But when the two armies clashed and the battle's landscape changed completely with the assistance of angels, he immediately changed his stance and cried out: "Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I see what you do not see; indeed, I fear Allah. And Allah is severe in penalty." We clarified in the commentary of Surah Al-Anfal that the Battle of Badr was brought about through the conspiracy of the Jews. It was they who incited the Quraysh, drew up the plan for them, and assured them of their support. But when the actual moment arrived, they disassociated themselves like Satan.

(The Known Method of Satanic Leaders): It is clarified in various places in the Quran that on the Day of Judgment, when criminals try to place the responsibility for their crimes on their satanic leaders, the latter will plainly reply: "You yourselves were transgressors; we had no authority over you to make us responsible for your crimes. Whatever you did, you did yourselves; now suffer the consequences." In this world too, the method of Satan's agents is to incite people to crimes, but when the consequences appear, they try to save themselves from any responsibility.

("So the outcome for both of them is that they will be in the Fire, abiding eternally therein. And that is the recompense of the wrongdoers.") (17).

That is, the attempt to save oneself and blame the other after committing the crime benefits neither party. Instead, both are cast into Hell forever. Such is the end of those who wrong their own souls.

(Note 1: Qala [he said] comes in various meanings, such as speaking, explaining, gesturing, inviting, inciting, and prompting to action.)

6. Content of Verses 18 to 24:

These are the concluding verses of the Surah. Although the address is grammatically directed to all Muslims, the target is specifically the hypocrites. They are warned that the most serious issue for every individual to contemplate is what they have sent forward for tomorrow, the Day of Account. Allah is well-aware of every person's words and deeds. He will recompense everyone for each action. Along with this, they are warned not to forget Allah like the Jews did. Those who forget Allah do not harm Allah in the least; rather, they become entirely blind to their own good and bad. Remember, the difference between the people of Hell and the people of Paradise will not be a minor one that can be ignored through complacency; rather, it will be a vast difference. Success and triumph will belong only to the people of Paradise. For others, there is nothing but eternal loss.

Then they are informed that as far as the completion of proof (itmaam al-hujjah) is concerned, no deficiency has been left that would allow people to present any excuse before Allah. This Quran being recited is such a powerful thing that if it were sent down upon a mountain, it would have humbled itself and split asunder from the fear of Allah despite its hardness and solidity. Those whose hearts are not moved by it should remember that this is not a shortcoming of the Quran, but rather the hardness of their own hearts that do not soften. Such hard-hearted people will not be affected by anything.

Finally, the mirror of Allah's attributes is placed before people so they may stand before it and evaluate their present and future, their good and evil, their beliefs and actions, and their ultimate end. They should align and beautify their outer and inner selves according to the demands of their Lord's attributes, lest they turn their existence into a curse for themselves. Recite the verses in this light.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses:

"O you who have believed, fear Allah. And let every soul look to what it has sent on for tomorrow - and fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do." (18)

(Reminder of the Day of Account): Although the address is to general Muslims, the context shows that it is primarily directed at the hypocrites whose weaknesses have been under discussion from the beginning of this Surah. At the end, they are directed toward the ultimate reality, the forgetfulness of which is the root of all spiritual diseases, and the remembrance of which is the sole cure for all intellectual, spiritual, and moral ailments. It is said: O you who believe! The necessary demand of your faith is that you fear your Lord and constantly evaluate your deeds to see what preparation you have made for tomorrow, the Day of Account. The repetition of the command to fear Him is to prevent anyone from falling into the misconception that this world is a mere plaything that will end without consequence. It will not end aimlessly; rather, recompense and punishment are inevitable. The Day of Resurrection is referred to by the word "tomorrow" (ghad) to point to its proximity and certainty, suggesting that one should not procrastinate in accounting for one's deeds by thinking that many days remain. It should be treated as something close, just as tomorrow follows today. "And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do." To emphasize the gravity of the matter, "fear Allah" is repeated, followed by a reminder of the reality that you should not assume God is unaware of any action of yours. He is aware of every word and deed. Therefore, safety lies in acting with the awareness that your deeds remain in His knowledge, and you will receive recompense or punishment one day.

("And be not like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves. Those are the defiantly disobedient.") (19).

It is said: do not become like those who forgot God, so He made them oblivious to their own end. "Like those who forgot Allah" refers to the Jews. The Jews were the mentors of these hypocrites. The Quran warns them that if they also forget God, they will not harm God in the least, but will only destroy themselves.

(Those Who Forget God Forget Themselves): In "so He made them forget themselves," an important reality is highlighted: when people forget God, the consequence is that they become entirely negligent of their own good, evil, and ultimate end. The entire value, nobility, and beauty of life depend on understanding the reality that the Creator did not make it for a few days of worldly enjoyment, but so that human beings, by living under God's commands, might make themselves worthy of an eternal kingdom. This ascension is achieved only by those who constantly remember that this life was not given by chance, but was bestowed by a Giver for a specific purpose. If lived according to this purpose, it is an immense eternal blessing; if the purpose is forgotten, it automatically becomes an eternal curse.

"Those are the defiantly disobedient." It is stated that these are the truly disobedient. Having forgotten God, they also forgot the nature of their life's relationship with God, resulting in their complete disobedience to Him. This also clarifies the reality of remembering God: its true spirit is that a person constantly keeps before them the nature of the relationship between themselves and their Lord. It is through this awareness that remembrance influences life. If this is missing, remembrance bears no fruit and becomes merely a formal ritual, the presence or absence of which is equal.

(A Highly Important Reality): "Not equal are the companions of the Fire and the companions of Paradise. The companions of Paradise - they are the attainers [of success]." (20)

This points to another highly important reality, which must be kept in view to understand the true value of life. That is, the difference between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell is not something to be taken lightly. If someone, overcome by desires, does not currently perceive the difference between Paradise and Hell, they must remember that the two do not differ merely by degrees, such that one could content oneself with the life of the people of Hell to some extent. Rather, the difference between the two is that of eternal punishment and eternal mercy. Success and triumph will belong only to the people of Paradise. For the people of Hell, there is nothing but curse and torment.

("If We had sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled and splitting asunder from fear of Allah. And these examples We present to the people that perhaps they will give thought.") (21)

(An Elegant Metaphor): This is a rebuke to the hypocrites, indicating that as far as teaching, reminding, and the completion of proof are concerned, Allah has left no deficiency. This Quran is such a powerful thing that if it were sent down upon a mountain—with all its hardness and insensitivity—it would have bowed down and split asunder out of fear of Allah. Yet, you are so hard-hearted and insensitive that your hearts are not moved by it in the least. "And these examples We present to the people that perhaps they will give thought." It is stated that We present these examples so people may reflect on their condition: if such an effective thing does not impact their hearts, the fault lies not in the object, but in the hardness of their own hearts. They should also consider that when the hardness of their hearts reaches such a degree, Allah, in accordance with His law, might seal their hearts just as He sealed the hearts of the Jews. This theme has been expressed in different styles elsewhere in the Quran. We present a few examples to bring the true perspective into view. In Surah Al-Baqarah, regarding the Jews, it is said:

"Then your hearts became hardened after that, being like stones or even harder. For indeed, there are stones from which rivers burst forth, and there are some of them that split open and water comes out, and there are some of them that fall down from fear of Allah. And Allah is not unaware of what you do" (Al-Baqarah: 74).

Similarly, in Surah Al-Hadid, regarding these very hypocrites under discussion, it is said:

"Has the time not come for those who have believed that their hearts should become humble for the remembrance of Allah and what has come down of the truth? And let them not be like those who were given the Scripture before, and a long period passed over them, so their hearts hardened; and many of them are defiantly disobedient" (Al-Hadid: 16).

From some indications of the respected teacher (may Allah have mercy on him), it appears that he takes this verse in the light of: "Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, and they declined to bear it and feared it; but man [undertook to] bear it. Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant" (Al-Ahzab: 72). There is no doubt that one aspect of it is clarified in this light, but the aforementioned verses of Al-Baqarah and Al-Hadid clarify its every dimension.

The Quran has described this as a similitude (tamtheel). In a similitude, there is no question of exaggeration or mere imagination; rather, it is observed how a spiritual reality is presented in a material form. From this perspective, this is a highly elegant and eloquent similitude. In "you would have seen it," the address can be general, but if we consider the addressee to be the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), this verse contains great solace for him: if these people are not moved by your call, it is not due to any shortcoming on your part. The Quran you recite to them is something that, if sent down upon a mountain, would have humbled and split it asunder out of fear of Allah, but their hearts are harder than stone, which is why they receive no impact.

("He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful.") (22)

(Reference to the Divine Attributes): When the fear of Allah was mentioned in the preceding verse, His beautiful names—which serve as fundamental names—are mentioned in this and the following verses, so that believers, disbelievers, and hypocrites may all know the attributes of the Lord of whom they are warned. They should know how to fear Him, how much to love Him, what to hope from Him, how to trust Him, and how to live and die in His path. We have clarified throughout this book that the foundation of the entire religion and Shariah rests upon the attributes of Allah and their demands. Everything expressed in the Quran, from Al-Hamd (the opening) to Al-Nas (the end), is a manifestation of the lights of the Divine attributes and their demands. The philosophy of religion emerged from them, and the forms of religion became visible through them. Therefore, understanding them is necessary, but to understand them fully, one must understand the entire Quran. Here, we will briefly point to the aspects that help in understanding the basic requirements of these attributes.

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity." First, He reminds of His divinity, and along with it, the fact that there is no other deity besides Him. Therefore, in times of both hope and fear, servants must turn to Him alone. No one else deserves to be taken as a deity, worshipped, or looked to as a source of hope.

"Knower of the unseen and the witnessed." He knows both the hidden and the manifest. The word "unseen" (ghayb) is used here relative to the servants; otherwise, for Allah, everything is in the category of "witnessed" (shahadah - present). Within this attribute lie aspects of both hope and fear. The aspect of fear is that whatever man does, whether secretly or openly, is in Allah's knowledge, and since everything is in His knowledge, He will inevitably call him to account. No one will be able to hide their words or deeds, nor will any intercessor be able to misrepresent facts before Him. The aspect of hope is that when the servant knows his Lord is aware of his hidden and manifest states, he should trust Him completely and present every request before Him alone.

"He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." We have discussed the difference between Rahman and Rahim under "In the name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." The attribute Rahman carries the concept of the intensity of mercy, while Rahim carries the concept of its permanence. If one reflects, both hope and fear are present in these attributes. The aspect of hope is clear: when God is both Rahman and Rahim, there is no fear of injustice or unfairness from Him that would require an intercessor to protect one from Him. Whatever He has done and will do is out of mercy, both in this world and the Hereafter. The aspect of fear is that because God is Merciful, He will not tolerate injustice to go unpunished; rather, He will inevitably take retribution for the oppression of the weak. This is why He has based the Day of Resurrection upon His attribute of mercy in various places in the Quran, stating that it is the necessary demand of His mercy to establish the Day of Judgment to do complete justice: "He has decreed upon Himself mercy. He will surely assemble you for the Day of Resurrection" (Al-An'am: 12).

("He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him.") (23)

In this verse, the segment that passed in the preceding verse is repeated first, showing that the negation of polytheism (shirk) and the affirmation of monotheism (tawhid) are of paramount importance here. The reason is that all deviation regarding the Divine attributes stems from polytheism. Since the entire religion and Shariah are based upon these attributes, it was necessary to block every possibility of deviation at this foundation. After this, eight of the beautiful names of Allah are mentioned in immediate succession, without any coordinating conjunctions. We have explained this rule of Arabic grammar elsewhere: when attributes appear without conjunctions, it signifies that all these attributes exist in the described subject simultaneously.

"The Sovereign" (Al-Malik): That is, He is the King who created this world, and He is its sole Owner and Ruler without partner. Based on this right, He sent His messengers to inform His servants of His commands, so they might obey them and gain the pleasure of their true King. "The Pure" (Al-Quddus): He is free from every defect, flaw, evil, and deficiency. For this reason, He sent down the Book and messengers to purify His servants, so they might become worthy of closeness to Him. In Surah Al-Jumu'ah, references to both "Sovereign" and "Pure" are made, and their requirement is clarified. First, His attributes are referenced: "the Sovereign, the Pure, the Exalted in Might, the Wise" (Al-Jumu'ah: 1). Then, the requirement of these attributes is clarified: "It is He who has sent among the unlettered a Messenger from themselves reciting to them His verses and purifying them" (Al-Jumu'ah: 2). Reflect on this: He sent the messenger and the Book because He is the Sovereign—it is the necessary requirement of His sovereignty that He send His envoys and commands to His subjects. And He desired their purification because He is the Pure; He does not like His servants to remain soiled with sins. The requirements of the attributes Aziz (Exalted in Might) and Hakim (Wise) are also stated, the explanation of which will come in their proper places, if Allah wills.

"The Perfection/Giver of Peace" (Al-Salam): This carries the meaning of safety, peace, and security. When wishing peace to someone, we use this word. In the Quran, regarding the Night of Decree, it is said: "Peace it is until the emergence of dawn" (Al-Qadr: 5), meaning Allah is a refuge and shield for His servants against every calamity and danger. When a servant places themselves in His protection, they find peace and tranquility. It is the effect of this attribute that His remembrance grants peace and tranquility to hearts: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured" (Ar-Ra'd: 28).

"The Bestower of Faith/Giver of Security" (Al-Mu'min): This means the Giver of security. When a servant seeks His refuge to escape the attacks of Satan and his agents, He takes them into His protection. This security cannot be obtained anywhere else. Satan's reach extends everywhere except to the shelter of God. "The Overseer/Guardian" (Al-Muhaymin): According to Khalil and Abu Ubaydah, it means the Guardian. According to Ibn al-Anbari, it means "the One who stands over people," i.e., their Protector. According to Imam Farahi, it means the Trustee and Advocate. In my view, there is no significant difference between these meanings, for the guardian and protector is indeed the trustee and advocate. The Quran is also described as muhaymin because it is the reliable criterion for all previous scriptures.

"The Exalted in Might" (Al-Aziz): The explanation of this has passed in various places. It carries the meaning of being beyond reach and inaccessible, as well as being dominant and powerful—no one can overcome Him, while He can defeat all. "The Compeller" (Al-Jabbar): This means the Powerful and Strong. In Arabic, this word is also used for palm trees that are exceptionally tall. In the Quran, it is used for the mighty people of whom the Children of Israel were afraid, saying to Moses: "Indeed within it are a people of tyrannical strength, and indeed, we will never enter it until they leave it; but if they leave it, then we will enter" (Al-Ma'idah: 22). This attribute negates all concepts of divinity in which primacy is given to minor deities. "The Superior" (Al-Mutakabbir): This means the One who possesses the consciousness of His own greatness and supremacy. If this feeling exists in anyone other than Allah, it is false, because no one possesses greatness that is inherent to themselves except Allah; any greatness others possess is bestowed by Allah. However, pride is beautiful and true for Allah because His greatness is inherent, eternal, and everlasting. It is the effect of this consciousness of His that He does not tolerate any partnership in His divinity and sovereignty. This consciousness of Allah is expressed in other scriptures as: "Your Lord God is a jealous God." Just as you do not tolerate your spouse being with another, He does not tolerate His servant worshipping another. In my view, the concept the Quran expresses with the word Mutakabbir is expressed in other scriptures as "Jealous" (Ghayur).

"Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him." That is, how far apart are these attributes of beauty and perfection of Allah from the false deities of the polytheists! What comparison can there be? The God who is characterized by these attributes is far too exalted to be associated with such things. If any such association is attempted, His other fundamental attributes would reject it, and if one tries to force it, incoherence would arise in His attributes.

("He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.") (24)

That is, He is Allah who carries out all stages of existence for everything. He designs the plan of everything, then He brings it into existence, and then He fashions its form and refines its details. In none of these stages does He seek help from anyone, nor can anyone assist Him. How then can anyone else become His partner in anything? Every existence passes through these three primary stages: the first is the design stage, for which the Arabic word is khalq; the second is bringing it into existence, for which the word is bar'; and the third is refining its shape and features, for which the word is taswir. Since Allah carries out all three stages for every object, He alone is entitled to all glorification and worship.

"To Him belong the best names." That is, these are the fundamental attributes that have been described, and He is the true possessor of all other excellent attributes. The word "names" (asmaa) here is used in the sense of attributes. All the names of Allah express one or another of His attributes. "Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him." Since Allah has carried out all stages of existence for everything, everything in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him. If man turns away due to the free will he has been granted, it is his ingratitude and rebellion. The proper attitude for him too is to glorify and worship his Lord alone and not associate any partner with Him.

"And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." At the end, He reminds once again of those attributes with which the Surah began. The meaning is that man must remember that Allah is Aziz (Exalted in Might), so He can do whatever He wills. If He had willed, He could have compelled man to worship and glorify Him just as the rest of creation does. But He is also Hakim (Wise), so He willed to test man by granting him free will, to see whether he recognizes his Lord's right or becomes a follower of Satan. By the grace of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. Praise be to Allah for this. (Rahman Abad. February 3, 1978. 23 Safar 1398 AH).
